serving.natural_language_portion: One standard fried egg (approximately 46 g, equivalent to one large hen egg cooked in a small amount of oil).

nutrient_source.title: Egg, whole, cooked, fried — USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy (NDB No. 01128)
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: USDA SR Legacy entry for whole hen egg, fried, used as the primary nutrient reference. Values are per 100 g edible portion. This is the closest approved-source match for a plain fried egg as served in Singapore hawker and casual dining contexts.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008 — Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC
gi_source.organization: Diabetes Care (peer-reviewed journal) / University of Sydney GI Database
gi_source.source_match_notes: Eggs contain negligible digestible carbohydrate (0.8 g per 100 g). The University of Sydney GI Database and peer-reviewed literature consistently classify pure protein-and-fat foods such as eggs as having an effectively unmeasurable GI. Per the zero-calorie beverage glycemic rule convention, glycemic_index is set to 1 (lowest valid positive value) to satisfy validator requirements; gi_category is 'low'. glycemic_load_per_serving = (1 × 0.37 g carbohydrate per 46 g serving) / 100 = 0.0 (rounded). This is consistent with Atkinson et al. 2008 and Brand-Miller classification.

hypertension.guidance: A single fried egg (46 g) contributes approximately 95 mg sodium; individuals with hypertension should monitor total daily sodium intake and limit added salt or high-sodium accompaniments when consuming fried eggs.

type2_diabetes.guidance: Fried eggs contain less than 1 g carbohydrate per 100 g and have an effectively negligible glycemic impact, making them a suitable protein source for individuals managing Type 2 Diabetes, though the cooking oil used adds fat calories that should be accounted for in total energy intake.

cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Fried eggs contain approximately 401 mg cholesterol and 4.0 g saturated fat per 100 g; individuals with cardiovascular disease should limit egg consumption to current clinical guidelines (typically up to 1 egg per day) and prefer cooking methods that minimise added saturated fat.

llm_training.natural_language_description: A fried egg is a whole hen egg cooked by cracking it onto a lightly oiled hot pan and frying until the white is set and the yolk is cooked to the desired doneness — sunny side up (yolk runny, white fully set), over easy (flipped briefly, yolk still runny), or over hard (yolk fully cooked). In Singapore, fried eggs are an extremely common and affordable side dish served alongside noodle soups, rice dishes, and kaya toast breakfasts. The egg white is firm and slightly crisp at the edges, while the yolk ranges from creamy and liquid to fully set depending on preparation. Nutritionally, a fried egg is a rich source of high-quality complete protein (13.6 g per 100 g), fat (14.8 g per 100 g), and micronutrients including vitamin B12, vitamin D, selenium, choline, and vitamin A. Carbohydrate content is negligible (0.8 g per 100 g), making it glycemically inert. The cholesterol content (approximately 401 mg per 100 g) has historically raised cardiovascular concerns, though current evidence suggests moderate egg consumption does not significantly increase cardiovascular risk in healthy individuals. For individuals with Type 2 Diabetes, fried eggs are a low-glycemic protein option. Sodium content is moderate and primarily derived from the egg itself plus any added salt during cooking.
common_user_queries: How many calories in a fried egg?
How much protein in a fried egg?
Is fried egg high in cholesterol?

confidence_reason: Nutrient values sourced directly from USDA SR Legacy (NDB 01128), which is an approved primary source for this food type. The fried egg is a well-characterised food with consistent nutrient profiles across approved databases. GI classification is well-supported by peer-reviewed literature confirming negligible glycemic impact of eggs.
notes: trans_fat_g is 0.0 — confirmed zero per USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy; eggs contain negligible industrially produced trans fats. dietary_fibre_g is 0.0 — naturally zero as eggs contain no plant-based fibre. vitamin_c_mg is 0.0 — confirmed zero per USDA FoodData Central; eggs do not contain vitamin C. glycemic_index is set to 1 (effectively zero) as eggs contain negligible digestible carbohydrates and have no measurable glycemic response; gi_category is low per convention consistent with University of Sydney GI Database classification of protein-fat foods. monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, potassium_mg, calcium_mg, iron_mg, cholesterol_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, and water_g are all reported from USDA SR Legacy NDB 01128. Serving size of 46 g represents one large hen egg without shell, consistent with USDA standard serving. Food is served at Hock Kee Braised Beef Noodles as a side add-on at SGD 1.10.
